speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, survivor Norbert Friedman, interviewer Mark Goldberg. We're in West Hempstead, New York, in the USA. Language is English. OK, you've had a couple minutes to think. Why don't you comment on that question, to what do you owe your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I don't owe it to guile. I know I do not owe it to any special skills, any wisdoms, or any toughness. Therefore, I can only come to one conclusion that I survived in order to give witness. I have no special other things to contribute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would-- why would I be chosen to survive? And giving witness has been a-- a-- a task that I have been involved in with for the last 27 years. I've spoken in my congregation at the time before the floodgates of silence have been lifted and-- and-- and the outpouring of information came, you know, from the lips that were sealed by the horrors. But I used to speak about it 27, 28 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to-- to congregations, to children, to schools. And I didn't speak in search for-- for sympathy or compassion for myself. I did not even speak to gain honor for the victims. I spoke in order to draw lessons for what we have experienced. Because I firmly believe that everything that man encounters can be of benefit for his future actions. And lessons can be drawn from it. And unless we draw lessons from that tragedy, that tragedy was in vain. And the lessons that I try to draw from it is the lessons of human compassion for one another, respect for human life. Because human life, once taken, is irrevocable. You cannot get it back. I-- I-- I speak and I try to teach people the lessons of tolerance. Because demonizing somebody, denigrating, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] describing him as-- as-- as unworthy is the first step to it's hurting that person. I-- I-- I try to draw a lesson that life is more precious than anything else, that human dignity is sacred. I try to teach the young people that I speak to to emphasize the beauty of those who are different, not to look for negative aspects, but always to emphasize and to-- to-- to cherish that which is different and the beautiful in that which is different. Because that way, they enrich themselves. I try to teach them that in order for them to be able to request pardons from others, they have to show tolerance to the other soul. How much good I can do, I don't know. But the letters that I receive from children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the pledges and the expressions of promise that they shall look differently in others from now on, and they-- that the lessons that I teach them leave a-- a-- a-- an imprint on them gives me hope. And they give me hope. It gives me also promise that my children may be able to live in a better world.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Thank you very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, please introduce your wife to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife Marilyn, who for 40 years had to put up with the idiosyncrasies that govern the life of a survivor. And I'd better let her tell you about all the problems that she had to suffer with.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I'd like to say that as a young girl growing up in the United States, an American girl, and even though I was embraced by loving and wonderful parents who taught me Yiddishkeit, I never, never would have been, I believe, the compassionate and tolerant individual that I have become were it not for my husband Norbert, whom I and my children revere and worship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Norbert is a very unusual, extraordinary man. And we all have gained from living, knowing, and being close to him. My children and all our friends [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] have always told me how lucky I am to be the wife of such an unusual, caring man who has taught us that material things mean nothing, that people are the most important things in the world. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're not supposed to exaggerate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I am. And those who know me, know I'm-- I am not exaggerating. There are many, many more things I can say. However, I-- I do believe that you will understand what I mean and what I've said will describe Norbert. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Another [INAUDIBLE] job, Sweetheart.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about his experience? Was this something he told you easily, or how did this happen? How did you come to know about his experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because from the very first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] meeting, Norbert told me about his life and what a unusual father he had and what an unusual mother he grew up with. And from the very, very early stages of my children's life, they knew about the Holocaust. My husband never concealed anything. They grew up knowing that their father was a Holocaust survivor. And I believe that he instilled in them the kindness and the tolerance that they have now for humanity. He was very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should say he-- he wanted his children to know about their grandfather and about their grandmother and about all their cousins and uncles that they would never see. And they have grown up knowing that their father is a very unusual man. And I believe that they will be and are very unusual and gifted children because of their relationships with their father and his very wonderful friends who have become our families. My children know that wherever they go, whether it be Europe, California, any part of the world where our friends live, they're embraced by them. And their children come here and we embrace them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother and father at the time of their engagement, probably someplace around 1920 or 1919. My mother's name was Golda. Her maiden name was Bochner-Mandelbaum and my father's name was Josef, Josef Friedman. At that time, he was serving in the Polish Army. Later on in my young years, I used to see him in the Polish uniform twice a year when he was called to go to maneuvers and used to come home in the Polish Army uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken in Krakow. I-- I-- I did not own the picture. The picture-- a distant relative living in Israel, a cousin of my mother, had that picture, and I-- I acquired the picture through them. This is the picture of my fourth grade classmates from the Jewish public school in the town of Bielsko. This is taken towards the end of the year. Out of the 42 students and the teacher in that picture, the four others besides myself that have survived the war. I know there were about three of them. Two of them are in Israel, and one lives in Montreal, Canada. I am in the middle of the-- somewheres in the middle of the picture, number 16 in the white shirt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know I got the center-- to be the center of this particular picture. This particular teacher didn't care much for me. I spoke too much in the class. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a list of name of the students in various classes in the vocation-- the Jewish vocational school, which was an annex to the Hebrew gymnasium in Krakow. My class consisted of about 20-some students. Only one besides myself have survived the war. He-- he-- he lives presently in Brooklyn as a member of the Krakow Friendship Society. This was a three-year vocational school where Jewish students learn mechanical trades and were able to go into the trade world with skills that otherwise they-- they would obtain several years of apprenticeship. And many of these students immigrated to Israel, and some of them survived. Most of them did not. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself and four of my Polish friends taken in 1941 in the village of Zawichost. Two of those fellows were members of the underground. The one standing on my immediate left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the middle, again. The one standing next to-- on my left, his name was Josef [? Kowalski, ?] the name that I used for my father for his Aryan papers. He was a son of wealthy landowners and he offered to hide my brother, my little brother over there, the offer which my mother declined out of fear that they would convert him to Christianity. They were good friends of mine. They lived in the same village. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of them did not survive the war. And one of them on the extreme right, that was another friend of mine. And they held up a German transport train and they were cut down by machine gun fire. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] This document was issued by the UNNRA tracing division upon my request. I requested some documentation of the fact that I have been in camps. I needed it for-- as a-- to substantiate my claims to the restitution office in Germany, when I asked for what's known as Wiedergutmachung, which is restitution. I was going-- attending college at that time, and I needed funds to-- to sustain myself. And this certified the fact that I have arrived in Dachau on a certain date and then I left for Natzweiler. This became an official document. The back of it is certified by the IRO, International Refugee Organization. If I'm not mistaken, gives my [INAUDIBLE] number, 118250. This was the number which I wore on-- over my breast underneath the Star of David from made out of yellow and a red triangle. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] To make more complicated. I can move there. This particular document, this is-- is the backside of the-- of the document that we showed before. That's the certification by the International Refugee Organization that the document is genuine. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This is a KZ-Ausweis issued by German authorities to ex-prisoners of concentration camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This document was issued in February of 1948. It attests to the fact that I have been in the ghettos of Krakow and Tarnow and the concentration camps of Flossenburg, Dachau, Mielce, and-- some-- and Pustkow. This particular document was useful in obtaining privileges and going to the theater and in-- in obtaining ration cards. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm-- I'm glad I was able to save it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, one moment. Wait a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took this picture in 1949 to give to my father when I visited him in Paris before I immigrated to United States. Today, I use it to prove that once I had hair. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This picture was taken in October of 1994 at the wedding of my youngest son David, who's standing behind my wife. My son Gary--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slow down. Slow down. Slow down. That's David, OK? Now, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, Gary Steven, standing behind me. I forgot to-- I failed to mention that my son Gary graduated Yeshiva University Law School with high honors and has dedicated himself to the spirit of law in all his endeavors. And same as-- as I, myself, dedicated myself to social issues, so as he and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the woman in the center?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman in the center is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me her full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marilyn [? Myasha ?] Friedman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was also taken at, obviously, at the wedding of my youngest son, his bride, Dr. Julie [? Sweig ?] Friedman is standing next to him. And on the other side is my other daughter-in-law, [? Noelle ?] Friedman, my son Gary's wife. And it's a picture of very educated people. There are four degrees there. Gary's an attorney. [? Noelle ?] has master's degrees from Columbia in health. She's accomplished artist. Julie graduated magna cum laude from medical school. She was accepted to Alpha Omega Alpha Medical Honor Society, of which I'm very proud. The only ones without degree are myself and my wife. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're all happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandson, Colin Daniel, the son of Gary and [? Noelle. ?] He lives in Santa Fe. He's presently about a year and a  half old. And he's the joy of our life and the promise of our future. [PAUSES FOR 40 SECONDS] This is the issue that graciously printed my letter to the to the editor dealing with special viewing of that was for the survivors who were actually members of that list. That particular picture, we are privileged to view in the presence of Mrs. Schindler and of Poldek Pfeffenberger, who was instrumental in having this picture made, who by the way, was my gym instructor in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to be invited to the showing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not invited. Somebody else with extra tickets gave me those tickets. And I was present at the showing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This particular letter was written as a result of the the very profound emotions I felt viewing the picture and the gratitude that I felt for the people who were instrumental in having this picture made. The picture itself brought so many memories, because it was filmed in my hometown, and my familiarity with the streets and the houses made it much more-- that made the impact much more profound. As I told in the previous testimony, I was in the ghetto in-- during Yom Kippur of 1941. I forgot to mention it. I went to the ghetto in 1941 looking for relatives. And only one aunt, one sister of my mother was still alive, and she lived in the ghetto with her husband and her daughter. I tried to get a job for her with Schindler or with the Emalia factory or the brush factory. And because of the short amount of time that I could stay in the ghetto, I wasn't able to do it. Although friends promised me upon my leaving of the ghetto that they're going to be able to help, none of this was accomplished. And of course, my mother's sister with her husband and my cousin perished together with all the other members of my mother's family. There's only one surviving member still alive. It's my mother's brother's son, my cousin, who lives presently in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As a tribute to my mother, my brother, and 600,000 Jewish men and women and children who perished in the extermination camp of Belsitz. I have written an ode to Belsitz. 'Auschwitz, Buchenwald, Dachau, names so familiar-- now names of interest, where the curious, each for his or her reasons, go to view, to ponder and reflect and the few that are left go to mourn. They view the edifices of men's twisted cruelty and they pay homage before the erected obelisks. Mauthausen, Majdanek, Treblinka-- somehow in the still of the night, when the tormented souls of your habitants offer solace to each other in their nocturnal visitations, they have the comfort of the past days' coals of consolation. But not so you, Belsitz. You lie forlorn in the mountains of a hostile soil of eastern Poland, unwanted and forgotten by the world. Somewhere, where the long, lonely railroad track marks the paths leading to endless solitude, tracks of no return. Fields of wildflowers of unusual beauty cover the hallowed ground. Trembling birches and slender weeping willows sway in the eternal wind to solitary visitor to this [INAUDIBLE], this sacrosanct place. Like a possessed dervish does the wind perform spiral convolutions, coiling itself around the singular, deserted monument, the lonely [INAUDIBLE] marking the tortured demise of 600,000 of our beloved kin. Occasionally, this invisible but evident Ruach, the same one that was present when the almighty created heaven and earth and declared it to be good, that same Ruach, that same wind, turns its anger into a storm and in accord with the earth, upheaves the bleached bones of the victims crying for proper burial. And daily, the same gale that parted the waters when the 6,000 crossed the Red Sea, rains over the bones and ashes of the 600,000 martyrs that perished [NON-ENGLISH]. There are no songs of victory thanksgiving by Miriam and Moses, only the earth's piercing wailing of the innocent souls of little Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, merciful and merciless Ruach, were you there when they were driven to their martyrdom in the crowded cattle cars, in the sweltering heat of the Polish summer? Did you force yourself through the cracks of their living coffins? Did you give comfort to their trembling, emaciated bodies? Did you offer hope for their paralyzing fears? Did you touch the lovely face of my mother? Did she hold my little brother close to her? Do you remember the distant features of the best that the Polish Jewry had to offer, the signs of my hometown, Krakow? Did you turn your howling force into a gentle zephyr at the sight of the saintly faces of the wise and pious? Where did you carry the last cry of the indestructible fate? [NON-ENGLISH] Oh, ever-present Ruach, they say that you carried the flames from above the Temple Mount when it was destroyed, that you lifted them on the wings of the mournful and the weeping of the faithful. Yet you have left a spark of evil that fanned by ages of ignorance and hated, burst into an all-consuming Shoah. Oh, wind, do you weep? Do you whine in sorrow when you visit the places of destruction? Is there a special note in the tone of your lamentation when you visit Belsitz, that melancholy, deserted shrine of suffering and torture? Do you bemoan the abandonment of those souls by remembering their anguish? Oh, wind, at the seasons of morning, encircle them with your shroud of tender pity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Comfort them. Counsel them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fortunate, for the sake of their souls and the souls of all who perished, shall glorify and sanctify his holy name forever. They shall have the children of the future remember the children whose future was denied them and was terminated in the most cruel way. They shall bow their heads and resolve that Belsitz, though you might have been abandoned and neglected, you shall never be forgotten.'
